last_maintained: likely 2018, inferred from local repo metadata (`version = "0.5.1"` and package copyright `2016 - 2018` in `Cargo.toml`; the changelog has no explicit release dates)
overall_strengths: Strong at APK unpacking plus immediate, reproducible checks over manifest and decompiled Java.
Easy to extend for signature-style rules because `rules.json` and `config.toml` are externalized.
Good evidence payload for analyst review: file path, exact line(s), and a surrounding snippet; HTML output adds a source browser.

Particularly reliable for surface-level Android smells: exported components, debuggable/allowBackup flags, insecure WebView patterns, insecure SSL acceptance patterns, world-readable/world-writable file modes, obvious SQL concatenation, obvious weak crypto strings, hardcoded URLs/emails/IPs.
Permission-gated rules are a useful small refinement over plain regex because they avoid running some storage checks when the manifest does not request the required permission.
overall_limitations: No taint analysis. `forward_check` is not real forward data flow; it just searches the whole same file for a second regex after a first regex matched.
No method, class, or call-chain reasoning. Findings are text matches with file/line evidence only.
No inter-procedural, inter-component, or implicit-intent analysis.
No AST/CFG/DFG/SSA. Decompiled Java text is the only code representation used for rule matching.

Regex rules can overfire on third-party libraries because the analyzer excludes `classes/android`, `classes/com/google/android/gms`, and `smali`, but still scans many bundled libraries such as `com/google/common`.
Decompiler artifacts can affect both false positives and false negatives. The tool reasons over whatever `jd-cmd` reconstructs, not over the original source or bytecode semantics.
Some shipped categories are policy/advisory rather than direct vulnerabilities (`Large heap`, Base64 use, root detection, dangerous permission requests).
Certificate analysis is feature-gated and narrow: it only checks debug cert usage and expiration, even though the code already notices self-signed certificates as a TODO.
Native code, reflection-heavy behavior, dynamic code loading semantics, and runtime-only issues are largely out of scope.

The tool has a small amount of same-file co-occurrence logic and one capture-substitution pattern, but it never models sources, sinks, sanitizers, propagation across methods, or path constraints. Its output is useful as auxiliary priors for known API misuse and manifest exposure, not as a taint-analysis substitute.
